did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used an elastic-scattering spectroscopy (ESS) device that employed artificial intelligence algorithms to compare lesion readings with training datasets for classifying skin lesions as high or low risk for malignancy. The AI method involved a final architecture of 10 neural networks (including feedforward and modified ResNet-12 architectures) trained on spectral data to stratify melanocytic and nonmelanocytic skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is explicitly described as a 'prospective, multicenter clinical validation study' and a 'comparative effectiveness study.' It reports original experimental data from 614 lesions evaluated at 4 US investigational sites, including specific statistical results (sensitivity, specificity, AUROC) and methods for patient enrollment and device assessment, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Use of an elastic-scattering spectroscopy and artificial intelligence device in the assessment of lesions suggestive of skin cancer: A comparative effectiveness study
brief_summary: This prospective, multicenter clinical validation study evaluated the safety and effectiveness of a handheld elastic-scattering spectroscopy (ESS) device utilizing artificial intelligence algorithms for assessing skin lesions suggestive of cancer. The device, which compares spectral readings against a training dataset of over 10,000 scans using an ensemble of 10 neural networks, was tested on 614 lesions from 394 participants across four US sites. The primary evaluation compared the device's diagnostic performance against expert dermatologists, measuring sensitivity, specificity, and AUROC. The ESS device demonstrated high overall sensitivity of 97.04%, with no statistically significant difference compared to dermatologist sensitivity (96.45%, P = .8203). However, the device showed lower specificity (26.22%) compared to dermatologists (56.10%, P < .0001). The study concludes that the ESS device offers high sensitivity for detecting skin cancer, potentially aiding primary care clinicians in lesion assessment and reducing morbidity through expedited detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the melanoma subgroup sensitivity analysis (96.67%). The paper notes that the melanoma category includes other high-risk melanocytic pathologies such as severely atypical melanocytic nevi and abnormal melanocytic proliferations. Also referred to as 'high-risk, pigmented lesions' in the discussion.
Included in the BCC subgroup sensitivity analysis (97.22%). Listed as a leading cause of cancer and a nonmelanoma skin cancer.
Included in the SCC subgroup sensitivity analysis (97.01%). Listed as a leading cause of cancer and a nonmelanoma skin cancer.

Used as the comparator for malignancy risk stratification. The device categorizes lesions as 'Monitor' (negative/benign) or 'Investigate Further' (positive/malignant). The study assumes unbiopsied lesions enrolled by dermatologists were benign for specificity calculations.
Specifically analyzed as a subset of melanoma cases (>50% of biopsy-proven cases).
Defined by Breslow depth 0.2-0.8 mm, comprising <25% of biopsy-proven cases.

all_task_types.details: The AI model classifies skin lesions into two discrete diagnostic categories based on malignancy risk. The device outputs 'Monitor' or 'Investigate Further'. For 'Investigate Further' classified lesions, a spectral similarity score (1-10) is generated, but the primary task is binary classification of malignancy risk. The study evaluates sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV for this classification task.

datasets_used.name: ESS Device Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: An expansive databank of spectral scans of benign and malignant lesions from multiple healthcare settings, comprising lesions from various company-sponsored and independent investigator clinical studies. Used to train the AI algorithm (version 1.0, revised to 3.0) which compares current ESS lesion readings against this data.

use.training: Used to train an ensemble of 10 neural networks (including feedforward and modified ResNet-12 architectures) for classifying skin lesions as high or low risk for malignancy.
use.other: The device compares current ESS readings from the study lesions against this training dataset to generate diagnostic outputs.

datasets_used.name: Study Testing Group
datasets_used.description: A prospective, multicenter clinical validation cohort of 333 randomly selected lesions from 208 participants recruited from 4 US investigational sites between August 2018 and October 2020. Participants were adults aged 22+ with clinically suspicious lesions measuring 2.5-15 mm.
classes_used: High-risk lesions (biopsy-proven malignant: melanoma, BCC, SCC)
Low-risk lesions (unbiopsied, assumed benign based on dermatologist clinical assessment)

use.testing: Used to assess the safety and effectiveness of the ESS device by comparing device diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUROC) against expert dermatologists' clinical and dermatoscopic evaluations.

datasets_used.name: Study Cross-Validation Group
datasets_used.description: A cohort of 281 lesions from 175 participants recruited from the same 4 US investigational sites. This group was used to evaluate the device's ability to determine whether previously unseen lesions were benign or malignant using machine learning.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the device's machine learning performance on previously unseen lesions.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate sample size, mean, median, standard deviation (SD), quartiles, minimum, and maximum for continuous outcomes; and number and percentage for categorical outcomes.
To estimate the uncertainty for the mean of continuous outcomes and the percentage of categorical outcomes.
To calculate the 95% confidence intervals for sensitivity and specificity (binary correlated data).
To assess statistical significance in comparisons between the ESS device and dermatologists for sensitivity and specificity, with a significance threshold set at 0.05.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) curve
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the device and dermatologists.
To determine enrollment requirements based on assumed sensitivity (90%) and specificity (30%) to achieve specific CI widths.

hypothesis_testing.test: Two-sided P values (specifically for comparing sensitivity and specificity between the ESS device and dermatologists)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in diagnostic performance (sensitivity or specificity) between the ESS device and expert dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in diagnostic performance (sensitivity or specificity) between the ESS device and expert dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A P value of .8203 indicated no statistically significant difference in overall sensitivity between the device and dermatologists. A P value < .0001 indicated that the specificity of the dermatologists was significantly higher than that of the device. A P value of .0096 indicated a significant difference in specificity for biopsied lesions.

Testing Group (All Lesions).description: 333 randomly selected lesions from 208 participants. Evaluated against expert dermatologists' clinical and dermatoscopic evaluation.
Testing Group (All Lesions).notes: No statistically significant difference in overall sensitivity between device and dermatologists. Dermatologist specificity was significantly higher. Unbiopsied lesions assumed benign for specificity calculations.

Testing Group (Biopsied Lesions Only).description: 284 biopsied lesions from the testing group. Evaluated against dermatologist/dermatopathologist diagnosis.
Testing Group (Biopsied Lesions Only).notes: No statistically significant difference in sensitivity between device and dermatologists for biopsied lesions. Device specificity was significantly lower than dermatologists. Melanoma sensitivity included high-risk melanocytic pathologies.

Testing Group (Unbiopsied Lesions Only).description: 49 unbiopsied lesions from the testing group.
Testing Group (Unbiopsied Lesions Only).notes: Specificity for unbiopsied lesions was greater than for biopsied lesions. Histopathologic diagnoses were not obtained for these lesions.

Cross-Validation Group.description: 281 lesions from 175 participants. Evaluated device's ability to determine if previously unseen lesions were benign or malignant using machine learning.
Cross-Validation Group.notes: Specific quantitative diagnostic metrics (sensitivity/specificity) for this group were not explicitly reported in the main text tables, but the group was used to validate the device's machine learning performance on unseen data.

Subgroup Analysis (Biopsied Lesions).description: Sensitivity analysis for specific cancer types within the biopsied cohort.
Subgroup Analysis (Biopsied Lesions).notes: Melanoma category includes other high-risk melanocytic pathologies such as severely atypical melanocytic nevi and abnormal melanocytic proliferations. Melanoma in situ comprised >50% of biopsy-proven cases. Stage 1 melanoma comprised <25%. Stage 2 or greater comprised remaining cases (n=6).

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on an expansive databank of spectral scans of benign and malignant lesions from multiple healthcare settings (over 10,000 readings from over 2000 lesions). Algorithm version 1.0 was used initially, revised to version 3.0.
architecture_used.input_size: Elastic-scattering spectroscopy (ESS) spectral data (photon backscatter reflections) using over 40 different wavelengths of light ranging from 360 to 810 nm.

components: One feedforward neural network trained on segments of the training data set.
Two deep neural networks based on a modified ResNet-18 architecture, with inputs based on ESS spectral data instead of imaging data.
Seven other neural networks with different architectures and training set variations to represent high-risk melanocytic lesions, high-risk nonmelanocytic lesions, and low-risk lesions.
architecture_details.ensemble_logic: Raw output adjusted based on class distribution of the training set (adjusting for priors).
architecture_details.output: Binary classification ('Monitor' or 'Investigate Further') and a spectral similarity score (1-10) for positive cases.

training_details.training_data_source: Company-sponsored and independent investigator clinical studies.
training_details.training_data_composition: Three different types of training models based on different architectures and versions of training sets.

classification_or_output_head.additional_output: Spectral similarity score between 1 and 10 for 'Investigate Further' classified lesions, based on degree of resemblance to confirmed malignant lesions in training studies.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Deep neural networks based on modified ResNet-18 architecture
preprocessing_techniques: Spectral waveform preprocessing to minimize high-frequency noise
Wide wavelength range (360-810 nm) used to remove regions with low signal-to-noise ratio
conclusions: The elastic-scattering spectroscopy (ESS) device demonstrated high overall sensitivity (97.04%) in detecting skin cancer, with no statistically significant difference compared to expert dermatologists (96.45%, P = .8203).
The device showed significantly lower specificity (26.22%) compared to expert dermatologists (56.10%, P < .0001).
The device achieved high subgroup sensitivities for melanoma (96.67%), basal cell carcinoma (97.22%), and squamous cell carcinoma (97.01%).

The study concludes that the ESS device may assist primary care clinicians in assessing suspicious lesions, potentially reducing skin cancer morbidity and mortality through expedited and enhanced detection and intervention.
important_remarks: The study was funded by DermaSensor, Inc., the manufacturer of the ESS device, indicating a potential conflict of interest.
The device demonstrated significantly lower specificity (26.22%) compared to expert dermatologists (56.10%, P < .0001), suggesting a high rate of false positives.

Specificity calculations for unbiopsied lesions rely on the assumption that all lesions not biopsied by dermatologists were benign, which may introduce a biased specificity estimate of 100% for this subset.
The sample size for melanoma was limited (n=30), which the authors note must be considered in context of the reported sensitivity results.

The training dataset comprised over 10,000 readings from over 2,000 lesions from various company-sponsored and independent investigator studies, but specific details on the distribution of lesion types in the training set were not fully detailed in the results.
The study excluded lesions in inaccessible areas (mucosal, nail, acral) and lesions larger than 15 mm or smaller than 2.5 mm, limiting generalizability to these populations.
The device does not differentiate among malignant pathologies, only stratifying risk as high or low, which limits its utility for specific diagnosis.
The study design blinded investigators to device output, but did not assess the impact on clinical care pathways or referral patterns.
One participant was unable to complete the study due to a technical device error (uncharged battery), though no other adverse events were reported.
The AUROC for the device (0.773) was slightly lower than that of dermatologists (0.785) for all lesions, though sensitivity was comparable.
